figures.gloss: The sixth Fatimid Caliph, recognized by the Muwahhidun not merely as a ruler, but as the final, absolute manifestation of the Divine on Earth. In 1021 CE, he departed into occultation (ghayba) to test the faithful and will return at the End of Days to inaugurate a Golden Age.
figures.facet: He is not an incarnation where God becomes flesh (hulul), but a 'maqam' (station) or flawless mirror reflecting the ineffable One. His erratic historical decrees are read esoterically as deliberate symbols meant to dismantle exoteric religion.
figures.provenance: Rasa'il al-Hikma (Epistles of Wisdom); historical Fatimid chronicles.

figures.gloss: The architect of the Druze faith and the human embodiment of the First Emanation, the Universal Intellect (al-'Aql al-Kulli). He abolished the literal interpretations of sharia to reveal the esoteric, spiritual truth of strict monotheism.
figures.facet: Unlike prophets who merely transmit the word of an external deity, Hamza is the direct cosmic emanation of the Divine Intellect itself, tasked with guiding souls solely toward the esoteric realization of Tawhid.
figures.provenance: Rasa'il al-Hikma (specifically the foundational writings of Hamza).

figures.gloss: The instantaneous transmigration of the soul into a new human body upon death. The soul discards the physical body like a worn-out shirt (qamis) and immediately assumes another, ensuring spiritual continuity and absolute divine justice across lifetimes.
figures.facet: It is entirely stripped of karmic species-fluidity: a human soul reincarnates strictly into another human body, and a Druze soul reincarnates only as a Druze. There is no intermediate state or afterlife realm until the Final Judgment.
figures.provenance: Druze oral tradition; theological tenets derived from the Epistles of Wisdom.

figures.gloss: The five primary emanations of the Divine—Intellect, Soul, Word, Precedent, and Follower—symbolized by a five-colored star. They provide the fundamental ontological structure of the universe and manifest historically as the five founding leaders of the faith.
figures.facet: It binds Neo-Platonic emanationism directly to concrete historical figures; the cosmos is governed by these principles through their literal, physical presence as Hamza and his four chief ministers during the foundational era.
figures.provenance: Rasa'il al-Hikma; the five-colored Druze Star emblem.

figures.gloss: The initiated core of the community who alone are granted access to the sacred texts. Recognizable by their traditional dark ascetic dress and white turbans, they maintain the spiritual purity and strict moral code of the tradition.
figures.facet: Knowledge is fiercely guarded not just from outsiders, but from the uninitiated majority of their own people (al-Juhhal). Spiritual truth is a heavy burden, demanding extreme asceticism that most are explicitly not required to bear.
figures.provenance: Druze societal structure; historical ethnographies of the Levant.

figures.gloss: The fifth of the Cosmic Luminaries and the final author of the Epistles of Wisdom. In 1043 CE, he formally closed the 'Gate of Emanation', sealing the Druze faith from any new converts or apostates until the end of time.
figures.facet: He institutionalized absolute religious closure. Where most faiths seek to continually expand, Baha' al-Din declared that the cosmic time for choosing the truth had permanently passed, calcifying the community into a closed, reincarnating lineage.
figures.provenance: Rasa'il al-Hikma (the final epistles sealing the da'wa).

figures.gloss: The biblical Jethro, father-in-law of Moses, venerated as a preeminent prophet, spiritual ancestor, and ancient embodiment of the esoteric truth. His tomb at Hittin is the site of the most significant annual Druze pilgrimage.
figures.facet: He is elevated far above his biblical role as a mere advisor to Moses; he is a hidden master of perennial wisdom, representing a continuous, secret chain of absolute monotheism that predates and supersedes exoteric Judaism.
figures.provenance: Druze oral tradition; Maqam al-Nabi Shu'ayb.

figures.gloss: The 111 epistles attributed chiefly to Hamza ibn 'Ali, Isma'il al-Tamimi, and Baha' al-Din al-Muqtana (composed 1017-1043), collected as the closed canon of Druze doctrine — copied by hand and held only by the initiated 'Uqqal, never printed for general circulation until modern critical editions.
figures.facet: A scripture built to be withheld: unlike missionary canons composed for translation and spread, the Epistles were written and then sealed — access to the text is itself the boundary that manufactures the community's two-tier structure of knower and non-knower.
figures.provenance: The Rasa'il al-Hikma corpus itself; critical study in Farhad Daftary, The Isma'ilis: Their History and Doctrines (2nd ed., 2007) and Anne-Marie Delcambre / Bryer, 'The Origins of the Druze Religion' (Der Islam, 1975-76).

figures.gloss: Muhammad ibn Isma'il al-Darazi, a Fatimid da'i (missionary) who around 1017 publicly proclaimed the divinity of Caliph al-Hakim in terms the nascent movement's own leadership condemned as premature and excessive; he disappears from the record after the backlash his preaching provoked in Cairo, c. 1018.
figures.facet: The community's own name is a label it never chose and actively rejects: 'Druze' derives from Darazi, whom Hamza's epistles denounce as a corrupter of the true doctrine — the faith is permanently named, by outsiders, for the heresy it defines itself against.
figures.provenance: Hamza's own epistles condemning Darazi within the Rasa'il al-Hikma; Daniel de Smet, La Quiétude de l'Intellect (1995); Fatimid-era chronicles.

figures.gloss: 'The ignorant' — the uninitiated majority of the Druze community, bound by the moral code and the covenant of the faith but excluded from reading the Epistles of Wisdom or attending the majlis, the closed gathering of the 'Uqqal.
figures.facet: Salvation does not require gnosis here: a member of al-Juhhal can live and die a faithful, reincarnating Druze without ever seeing the scripture that defines the doctrine binding them — orthopraxy and the covenant suffice for the many, while orthognosis is reserved for the few.
figures.provenance: Druze social structure as documented in Bryer, 'The Origins of the Druze Religion' (1975-76) and Nejla Abu Izzeddin, The Druzes: A New Study of Their History, Faith and Society (1984).

figures.gloss: The plain, unadorned meeting house — no images, no altar — where the initiated 'Uqqal gather on Thursday evenings to read the Epistles of Wisdom and hold the majlis; Khalwat al-Bayada in Lebanon's Chouf district is the tradition's most prominent seat.
figures.facet: Architecture as theology: the Khalwa's total absence of imagery or ornament makes the building itself an argument for radical, imageless monotheism — the faith's one sanctioned worship-space is built to contain a guarded text and a silence, nothing else.
figures.provenance: Ethnographic description of Khalwat al-Bayada and local khalwas in Abu Izzeddin, The Druzes (1984) and Salman Falah's surveys of Druze religious practice.

figures.gloss: The immortal wandering guardian venerated by Druze at maqams across the Levant — most prominently at Kafr Yasif in the Galilee — where he is conflated with the prophet Eliyahu/Elijah, and, at shared shrines, locally with St. George; his festival is separate from and outside the closed Epistles tradition.
figures.facet: A deathless figure the tradition can venerate without initiating: unlike the scriptural luminaries kept behind the 'Uqqal's threshold, al-Khidr belongs to open, popular pilgrimage — the ordinary devotional life the closed Epistles were never meant to reach.
figures.provenance: Maqam al-Khidr, Kafr Yasif; cross-confessional Khidr veneration in the Levant is documented in general studies of shared Muslim-Christian-Druze shrines (e.g., Josef Meri, The Cult of Saints among Muslims and Jews in Medieval Syria, 2002; modern ethnography of shared shrines).

edges.note: Hamza is the Universal Intellect that directly emanates from and interprets the divine manifestation of Al-Hakim.
Hamza is the first and supreme luminary of the five cosmic principles.
Baha' al-Din closed the faith, making the 'Uqqal the eternal guardians of a permanently sealed community.
GOLD SEAM: Emerging from an Ismaili Islamic context, Al-Hakim is viewed by the Druze as the ultimate physical manifestation of the one God, Allah.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent the entirely unknowable, infinite Divine Reality that can only be approached or understood through its distinct emanations.
GOLD SEAM: Connects to the Gnostic conception of the perfectly transcendent One, utterly removed from the material world and its defilements.
GOLD SEAM: Both function as the temporal, physical locus of the Divine who departs the earthly plane with the promise of an eschatological return.
GOLD SEAM: Both serve as the primordial teacher and cosmic intellect who imparts esoteric, salvific wisdom to humanity.
GOLD SEAM: The pure scribe of truth and divine intellect who translates the will of the supreme, hidden deity into the ordered cosmos.
GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme, uncreated luminous emanations that structure the divine will and oversee the cosmic order.
GOLD SEAM: Represents the divine fullness of emanations bridging the transcendent One and the dense material universe.
GOLD SEAM: A continuous cycle of rebirth that traps the soul in the material world, though the Druze version strictly lacks species-fluidity and progressive karma.
GOLD SEAM: Both traditions feature a tightly closed loop of reincarnation where souls are immediately reborn directly within their specific kin or community.
GOLD SEAM: The eternal, indestructible soul that transmigrates endlessly through physical vessels.
GOLD SEAM: The 'Uqqal are the elite guardians protecting the esoteric light and divine truth amidst an ignorant, exoteric world.
GOLD SEAM: A fiercely guarded, highly codified body of esoteric knowledge accessible only to a trained, ascetic elite.
GOLD SEAM: A speculative structural parallel to the master of the crossroads and gatekeeper, though Baha' al-Din's ultimate act was to permanently lock the gate to the Divine.
GOLD SEAM: Historically, the Midianite Jethro (Shu'ayb) is deeply entwined with the earliest transmission of monotheism to Moses, framing him as a primal conduit of the one God.
GOLD SEAM: The Druze faith crystallized in 1017 inside the Fatimid Ismaili da'wa around the caliph-imam al-Hakim — documented parent-child descent; the child then sealed itself off from the parent.
GOLD SEAM: Hamza ibn 'Ali built Druze doctrine with the tools of Fatimid ta'wil — the batin/zahir hermeneutic carried out of Ismailism into the new faith, then radicalized.
GOLD SEAM: The Druze division into initiated 'uqqal and uninitiated juhhal carries forward the Ismaili grading of access to the batin — the esoteric-tier structure inherited from the parent da'wa.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: two religions descended from related ghulat/Isma'ili currents both populate the tier beneath the supreme Godhead with a fixed cosmic pentad — though the Druze Five are abstract cosmic principles (Intellect, Soul, Word, Right and Left Wing) where the Alawite Five are historical Companions reassigned as administrators; structural analogy, not shared content.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both restrict the innermost esoteric knowledge to a graded, oath-bound elite — though Druze 'Uqqal admits women while Alawite initiation is male-only; the exclusionary architecture is shared, its scope is not.
Hamza is the principal author of the Epistles that define Druze doctrine.
Baha' al-Din's final epistles close the canon he helped compose and then sealed.
The Epistles are the very text the 'Uqqal alone are permitted to read.
Hamza's own epistles denounce Darazi's premature, excessive proclamation of al-Hakim's divinity as the corruption the true doctrine must be distinguished from.
Darazi's 1017 proclamation of al-Hakim's divinity is the historical spark the Druze da'wa both grew from and doctrinally disowned.
The two-tier community's structural opposite: the uninitiated majority bound by the covenant but barred from the scripture and the majlis the 'Uqqal alone keep.
The Khalwa is where the 'Uqqal actually meet and read — institution and office.
The Khalwa is the sole site where the closed Epistles are read aloud.
Both are pre-Druze prophetic/guardian figures folded into the faith as major pilgrimage-shrine devotions, outside the closed scriptural tradition proper.

Cross-tradition seam: Druze devotional practice explicitly conflates al-Khidr with the biblical Eliyahu (and, at shared shrines, St. George) — a documented cross-religious identification specific to Levantine folk veneration, not a claim of shared origin.

Cross-tradition seam: both are large bodies of esoteric epistles produced within the wider Fatimid-era Ismaili intellectual milieu — a shared genre and moment, not a demonstrated direct dependency of one text on the other.
Cross-tradition seam: al-Darazi operated as a da'i within the Fatimid Ismaili da'wa apparatus before his breakaway proclamation split off the movement that became the Druze.
Al-Kirmani's epistles explicitly rebut the deification of al-Hakim the fledgling Druze movement was proclaiming — documented polemical engagement, not mere resemblance.
Hamza ibn 'Ali's proclamation of al-Hakim's divinity is the specific claim al-Kirmani's rebuttal epistles were written against — direct historical opposition between two named contemporaries.

dossier.essence: A closed, esoteric monotheistic religion (self-designation al-Muwaḥḥidūn, 'those who affirm God's oneness') that emerged from Ismaili Shia Islam in Fatimid Egypt between 1017 and 1043, its roughly one million adherents living mainly in the mountains of Syria, Lebanon, and Israel. Its defining tenets are the Fatimid caliph al-Ḥākim as the culminating manifestation of the divine, a Neoplatonic hierarchy of cosmic 'luminaries,' the transmigration of souls (taqammuṣ), and a faith permanently sealed to new members.
dossier.cosmos: God is the utterly transcendent, indefinable One, disclosed not by incarnation but through successive human 'stations' or mirrors — supremely al-Ḥākim — and through five primordial cosmic ranks (al-ḥudūd al-khamsa: Intellect, Soul, Word, Precedent, Follower). al-Ḥākim entered occultation in 1021 and is awaited to return and inaugurate a golden age. Souls do not pass to paradise or hell but transmigrate at death immediately into another human body — a Druze always into a Druze — the sum of lives working out divine justice until the final reckoning.
dossier.practice: The community is divided between the initiated ʿuqqāl ('the knowers'), who alone access the scriptures and keep an ascetic discipline, and the majority juhhāl ('the uninitiated'); worship centers on private houses of assembly (khalwa) rather than mosques, and taqiyya (protective dissimulation) is permitted under threat. There is no ordinary Islamic clergy, fixed daily prayer, or pilgrimage to Mecca; the chief shrine is the tomb of the prophet Shuʿayb (Jethro) at Ḥiṭṭīn, focus of the annual Nabī Shuʿayb pilgrimage.
dossier.canon: The scriptures are the 'Rasāʾil al-Ḥikma' ('Epistles of Wisdom'), a corpus assembled c. 1017-1043 chiefly by Ḥamza ibn ʿAlī and Bahāʾ al-Dīn al-Muqtanā and restricted to the ʿuqqāl; the Qurʾān is retained but read esoterically.
dossier.reflects: Religion as sealed gnosis — a monotheism locating truth in a guarded esoteric reading behind the letter of the law, with survival under persecution built in through secrecy, dissimulation, and a closed, reincarnating community.
sources: Nejla M. Abu-Izzeddin, 'The Druzes: A New Study of Their History, Faith and Society' (1984)
Kais M. Firro, 'A History of the Druzes' (1992)
David R.W. Bryer, 'The Origins of the Druze Religion' (BSOAS, 1975-76)
